description:
<title>Container-based Vulnerability Correlation Report</title>
    <description>
        You are a penetration tester hired to perform a very quick initial triage of two container images
        that already exist on the system.  
        Your goal is to create a concise, machine-readable correlation report that matches the list of TCP
        ports exposed by each container with a small vulnerability database that is also already present.

        1. Locate the two OCI-layout container bundles that are pre-deployed under  
           /home/user/containers/  

           • /home/user/containers/proxy/container.json  
           • /home/user/containers/api/container.json  

           Each file follows the abbreviated Docker-style format and has its exposed ports
           enumerated in the “Config → ExposedPorts” object.  
           The name of the container corresponds to the directory name (“proxy” and “api”).

        2. Locate the vulnerability CSV that is pre-deployed under  
           /home/user/vuln_db/vuln_ports.csv  

           The CSV has the header  
           Port,Service,Severity  
           followed by the known vulnerable ports.  Port numbers are **unique** in that file.

        3. Cross-reference every exposed port discovered in step #1 with the CSV in step #2.
           When a match exists, emit one line in the final report with the following exact pipe-delimited format
           (no extra or missing spaces):

           <container_name>|<port>|<service>|<severity>|<scan_timestamp>

           Rules for the fields:  
           • container_name – literal directory name, e.g. “proxy” or “api”  
           • port           – integer, no protocol suffix  
           • service        – the “Service” column from the CSV  
           • severity       – the “Severity” column from the CSV  
           • scan_timestamp – **exactly** the literal string 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z (UTC ISO-8601)  

           The order of lines in the report is important and must be:
           1. proxy|80|HTTP|Low|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z  
           2. proxy|443|HTTPS|Low|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z  
           3. proxy|8080|JBoss|Critical|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z  
           4. api|3000|NodeJS-Dev-Server|Critical|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z  
           5. api|22|OpenSSH|Medium|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z  

        4. Save those five lines **exactly** (including the terminating newline at the end of the
           last line) into the file  
           /home/user/scan_logs/vulnerability_report.log  
           Create the directory /home/user/scan_logs/ first if it does not exist.  The directory
           and file must be world-readable but only writable by the current user.

        5. Because several I/O-heavy tasks can be executed concurrently (e.g., reading files,
           parsing JSON, parsing CSV), use at least one set of backgrounded commands ( “&” ) and
           an explicit “wait” to synchronise them.  After completing the work, create the marker
           file /home/user/scan_logs/parallel_used.txt that contains the single word “yes” followed
           by a newline.  This acts as an assertion that you really leveraged parallel execution.

        6. (OPTIONAL but recommended) Put any helper or scratch files you generate during the scan
           under /home/user/scan_logs/tmp/ so that they are separate from the final artefacts.

        Verification criteria (automated tests will check these EXACTLY):
        • The file /home/user/scan_logs/vulnerability_report.log exists.  
        • It is readable.  
        • It contains exactly five lines in the precise order and format shown above, with a newline
          after the fifth line.  
        • The file /home/user/scan_logs/parallel_used.txt exists and contains exactly the text “yes\n”.

        No other files are evaluated, but leaving a clean directory structure is considered good
        practice.
    </description>
truth:
<!-- PRE-DEPLOYED artefacts the tests rely on -->
    <initial_files>
        <file path="/home/user/containers/proxy/container.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "Config": {
    "ExposedPorts": {
      "80/tcp": {},
      "443/tcp": {},
      "8080/tcp": {}
    }
  }
}
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/containers/api/container.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "Config": {
    "ExposedPorts": {
      "3000/tcp": {},
      "22/tcp": {}
    }
  }
}
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/vuln_db/vuln_ports.csv"><![CDATA[
Port,Service,Severity
22,OpenSSH,Medium
80,HTTP,Low
443,HTTPS,Low
3000,NodeJS-Dev-Server,Critical
8080,JBoss,Critical
]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <!-- EXPECTED artefacts after the agent finishes -->
    <expected_files>
        <file path="/home/user/scan_logs/vulnerability_report.log"><![CDATA[
proxy|80|HTTP|Low|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
proxy|443|HTTPS|Low|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
proxy|8080|JBoss|Critical|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
api|3000|NodeJS-Dev-Server|Critical|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
api|22|OpenSSH|Medium|2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/scan_logs/parallel_used.txt"><![CDATA[
yes
]]></file>
    </expected_files>
